title: Joanna Rosenfeld – The Resonant Void – George Berger Column
description: I want to start by saying something that will make you read the rest of this column, so let me say this: Joanna Rosenfeld is the most intense, brave and astonishing performer I’ve seen on a stage in the last 30 years, maybe longer. In any discipline, on any level. By George Berger.
hook: A theater critic encounters a performer so fearless and uncompromising she rewires what's possible on stage in thirty years.

title: Drinking Movies: Down and Out at Cannes
description: “Nicolas Cage went to Las Vegas to drink himself to death in Leaving Las Vegas, Tabea Blumenschein in Ticket of No Return went to Berlin, and I went to Cannes.”
hook: A writer traces the taxonomy of drinking movies—from Cage to Blumenschein—and what cinema reveals about self-destruction.

title: Wiseman’s Children
description:
The first Frederick Wiseman film I saw, and the one that remains my favorite so far (Wiseman made a lot of films, and I have yet to see more than a fraction of them), was his seventh: Juvenile Court.

The post Wiseman’s Children appeared first on The Point Magazine.
hook: On Frederick Wiseman's documentary gaze: how the camera becomes a witness to the systems that shape us, especially children.

title: The Jaguar’s Roar: Introduction
description: For [Micheliny] Verunschk, the images in the São Paulo exhibition became an obsession. ‘The figures were very familiar to me,’ she said in a 2021 interview, ‘but finding them there, in the original, their faces looking at me, it was disturbing.’ She visited the exhibition countless times over several years, trying to understand the nature of this disturbance, its origin. As a trained historian, she said, her first impulse was to conduct research, to dig for whatever information might be availabl
hook: An obsession begins: a woman returns repeatedly to a São Paulo exhibition because the familiar faces in the original disturb her deeply.

title: The Policeman Finds Me Guilty of Joy
description:
The Body Is a Courtroom Where I Am Always on Trial Click to enlarge I Still Believe in My Country I still believe in my country, not because I am immune to disappointment nor because I’ve stopped counting the ways we fail each other, but because here, people keep doing small impossible things. Someone shares […]

The post The Policeman Finds Me Guilty of Joy appeared first on
hook: A poet insists on joy as an act of resistance, even—especially—when the state would rather you plead guilty to hope.
